1:
"Then came together unto him the Pharisees, and certain of the scribes, which came from Jerusalem." The witnesses dwell on the hostile errand of these visitors and the design of the controversy they provoke.

The Pharisees and scribes came not to learn but to accuse. Bede contrasts them with "the people of the land of Gennesareth," unlearned men who brought their sick to touch his garment, while these teachers "run together to the Lord, not to seek for healing, but to move captious questions." Henry's tradition notes they came "from Jerusalem down to Galilee — fourscore or a hundred miles, to pick quarrels," claiming "an authority over the country clergy"; "had they come so far to be taught by him, their zeal had been commendable; but to come... to oppose him... was great wickedness." Clarke adds that "malice and envy are never idle."

Their grievance was ceremonial. They saw some disciples "eat bread with common, that is, with unwashen hands, and found fault" — "common," Jerome explains, "is here put for unclean," the Jews calling "those meats common which all made use of." Theophylact notes the disciples "used to eat in a simple way, without washing their hands," and the Pharisees "took it up" — not as a breach of the law, but "for transgressing the traditions of the elders."

Henry's tradition sets the controversy in its larger frame: "one great design of Christ's coming was to set aside the ceremonial law which God made"; and "to make way for which he begins with the ceremonial law which men had made, and added to the law of God's making," discharging his disciples from its obligation. Pseudo-Jerome reads the scene mystically: "the disciples eating with unwashed hands signifies the future fellowship of the Gentiles with the Apostles" — "the cleaning and washing of the Pharisees is barren; but the fellowship of the Apostles, though without washing, has stretched out its branches as far as the sea." Henry's tradition adds that the Jerusalem scribes "pretended not only to a pre-eminence above, but to an authority over, the country clergy," keeping up "visitations" and sending "inquisitors" — so that their coming was an official campaign against the spreading gospel, not a chance encounter.
2:
"And when they saw some of his disciples eat bread with defiled, that is to say, with unwashen, hands, they found fault." The witnesses explain the term "defiled" and the nature of the objection.

The grammatical-historical commentators note that Mark, writing for Gentiles, explains a Jewish term. "Defiled," says Ellicott, "means literally common," a word that "came to be associated... with what was unclean" (cf. Acts 10:14, "common and unclean"), so "for Jews at all events the word acquired a new meaning"; "St. Mark's Gentile readers, however, were not likely to understand what was meant by common hands, and therefore he adds his explanation of unwashed." Robertson traces the same development: koinos "originally meant what was common to everybody," then "what is vulgar or profane," then "the ceremonially unclean."

The objection, the witnesses stress, was ceremonial, not sanitary. The emissaries "had seen some of the disciples eat without washing their hands" — Robertson suggests they may have been "seen eating some of the bread preserved in the twelve baskets" the day before, with "no particular opportunity to wash." Washing before meals is "a very proper thing to do... for sanitary reasons," he allows, "but the objection raised is on ceremonial, not sanitary, grounds." Barnes notes "the hands were considered defiled, or polluted, unless they were washed previous to every meal."

That "they found fault" the commentators take as the whole point of their coming. Clarke notes the clause is textually doubtful in some manuscripts, but the sense is plain: the spies had found their opening. The disciples, eating in the ordinary way, had unwittingly furnished the charge their watchers desired — a transgression not of any law of God, but of the rabbinic rule of ceremonial hand-washing. The fault-finding, the witnesses observe, reveals the spirit of the inquisitors more than any fault in the disciples: men who travelled far in search of an accusation, and seized on a matter of human custom to frame it. Gill notes the disciples were simply observed "to sit down to meat," and that the watchers "took notice" of the unwashen hands at once — an opportunity, he says, that "soon offered" itself to men who had come on purpose to find one.
3:
"For the Pharisees, and all the Jews, except they wash their hands oft, eat not, holding the tradition of the elders." The witnesses treat Mark's explanatory note, the disputed word "oft," and the tradition itself.

This is another note for Gentile readers. Ellicott observes that "the custom of which he speaks was not... peculiar to the Pharisees as a sect; it had passed, through their influence, to the whole body of the people." Gill notes the Pharisees were "very tenacious of this tradition of washing hands before eating," so much that they were described as those who "eat their common food with cleanness."

The word "oft" the commentators find a famous crux. The Greek (pygmē) means literally "with the fist," and the manuscripts vary. Lightfoot, from the rabbis, takes it of washing "the fist unto the joining of the arm," up to the wrist; Clarke renders "the hand to the wrist," citing the rabbinic rule and the parallel Muslim ablution; Barnes prefers "diligently, accurately, carefully"; Robertson explains "with the fist, up to the elbow, rubbing one hand and arm with the other hand clenched." Whether the sense is "frequently" or "with the fist," all agree it points to a scrupulous, prescribed ritual.

The "tradition of the elders" the witnesses define as custom handed down, not Scripture. It was, Barnes says, "what had been communicated from father to son, as being proper and binding," not "delivered by writing in the law of Moses" (cf. Galatians 1:14; Colossians 2:8). Henry's tradition adds that the elders "interposed their authority, and commanded all to do it upon pain of excommunication," making a matter "indifferent in its own nature" into a binding rule — "the Pharisees gained a reputation among the people for sanctity, and thus exercised and kept up an authority over their consciences" (cf. Matthew 15:2). The note thus prepares the reader to see the coming clash not as law against lawlessness, but as God's command against men's invention. Lightfoot adds from the rabbis the further refinement that "the second waters cleanse whatsoever parts of the hands the first waters had washed," a layered scrupulosity that shows how far the custom had been elaborated beyond anything Scripture required.
4:
"And when they come from the market, except they wash, they eat not. And many other things there be, which they have received to hold, as the washing of cups, and pots, brasen vessels, and of tables." The witnesses dwell on the market-defilement, the disputed verb "wash," and the endless multiplication of washings.

The market was a place of ceremonial peril. Robertson explains that "ceremonial defilement was inevitable in the mixing with men in public" at the agora; Ellicott adds the idea "was not one of cleanliness or health, but of arrogant exclusiveness" — they "might have come... into passing contact with a Gentile, and his touch was as defiling as if it had been that of a corpse." Henry's tradition recalls the spirit, "Stand by thyself... I am holier than thou."

The verb "wash" here (baptisōntai) the commentators discuss at length, for it is the word from which "baptise" comes. Ellicott notes it "implies the washing or immersion... of the whole body," as the former verb did of part; Robertson cites Meyer's climax — "before eating they wash the hands always; when they come from market they take a bath." Yet the witnesses do not press immersion universally: a Western reading has "sprinkle" (rhantisōntai), and Barnes argues from the parallel "baptism" of couches that the word "is used in the sense of washing in any way." Wesley likewise: "the Greek word means indifferently either washing or sprinkling."

The catalogue of washings the commentators take as proof of superstition's endlessness. "Cups, and pots, brasen vessels, and tables" were all scrupulously cleansed lest a heathen had touched them — the "pots" being the xestēs, a Latin loan-word (Ellicott, Clarke), and the "tables" rather "couches" on which they reclined (Ellicott, Barnes). Barnes notes the "baptism of tables" cannot mean entire immersion of couches, and so shows the word need not mean immersion; the witnesses observe that, having added one rule, the elders multiplied "many other things," for, as Henry's tradition says, "superstition is an endless thing" (cf. the divers washings of Hebrews 9:10). Once "one human invention... be admitted," Henry warns, "behold, a troop comes, a door is opened for many other such things" — the single rule of washing hands breeding the whole apparatus of cleansing pots, cups, vessels, and beds.
5:
"Then the Pharisees and scribes asked him, Why walk not thy disciples according to the tradition of the elders, but eat bread with unwashen hands?" The witnesses note the form of the challenge and the assumption behind it.

The question was aimed at Christ. Gill notes it was put "not to the disciples, but to Christ himself; for their chief view was to find fault, and quarrel with him." They appeal to him, Henry's tradition observes, expecting him to "check" his disciples and "order them to conform"; "they that are fond of their own inventions and impositions are commonly ready to appeal to Christ, as if his authority must interpose for the enforcing of them."

The wording is telling. They do not ask, "Why do not thy disciples do as we do?" — "though that was what they meant," says Henry's tradition, "coveting to make themselves the standard" — but "Why do not they walk according to the tradition of the elders?" The complaint is framed wholly in terms of the human rule, as if conformity to the elders were the measure of righteousness. Ellicott notes the better text reads "with defiled hands," the stronger word the Pharisees would naturally use, the milder "unwashen" being a transcriber's easing.

The "tradition of the elders," Wesley sums up, is "the rule delivered down from your forefathers." The witnesses find in the question the heart of the whole controversy: the Pharisees measured the disciples not against the law of God but against the accumulated customs of men, and counted departure from those customs a fault worthy of rebuke. To this Christ will answer, Henry's tradition notes, not by defending a breach of God's law — for there was none — but by exposing how the elders' tradition had itself become a means of breaking God's law; the very men who guarded the washing of cups had let slip the weightier commands. The disciples, "by receiving the doctrine of Christ, had more understanding than all their teachers" — and their liberty from the tradition was no laxity, but a truer obedience. Clarke simply refers the reader to the fuller treatment in Matthew, where the same demand is met; but the thrust is already plain — the question that was meant to convict the disciples will be turned, in Christ's reply, into a conviction of the questioners themselves.
6:
"He answered and said unto them, Well hath Esaias prophesied of you hypocrites, as it is written, This people honoureth me with their lips, but their heart is far from me." The witnesses note the order of the reply, the charge of hypocrisy, and the prophecy applied.

Mark, Gill observes, places the Isaiah citation first, where Matthew "postpones" it to follow the Corban argument; the substance is the same. The quotation is from Isaiah 29:13 (Barnes, Wesley). Robertson notes the opening "Well" (kalōs) is "appositely here," reserved for "ironical sarcasm in verse 9," and that Christ addresses them pointedly as "you hypocrites."

The charge of hypocrisy the fathers draw out. Pseudo-Chrysostom says Christ "sharply confounds them, calling them hypocrites, as looking with reverence upon what was not worthy of it"; their case is exactly that of Isaiah's people, who "honour God with their lips, whilst their heart is far from Him," vainly pretending "to observe the dictates of piety, whilst they honour the doctrines of men."

The lip-honour without the heart Henry's tradition makes the root of their fault. "They honour me with their lips," he paraphrases — "they pretend it is for the glory of God that they impose those things... but really their heart is far from God, and is governed by nothing but ambition and covetousness." They "rested in the outside of all their religious exercises, and their hearts were not right with God in them, and this was worshipping God in vain; for neither was he pleased with such sham-devotions, nor were they profited by them." The witnesses thus find in the prophet's ancient word the exact diagnosis of the scribes' religion: an elaborate outward observance covering a heart estranged from the God it claimed to serve — the very definition, as Christ names it, of hypocrisy (cf. Matthew 15:7). Clarke notes a variant in some copies reading "loveth me" for "honoureth me," but the sense is unaltered: a profession of the lips that the heart does not own is, whether called love or honour, no true worship at all. Ellicott notes the precise force of the verb — "well did Esaias prophesy" — pointing not merely to a happy coincidence but to a prophecy that found in these very men its exact and intended fulfilment.
7:
"Howbeit in vain do they worship me, teaching for doctrines the commandments of men." The witnesses dwell on the vanity of such worship and the substitution of men's rules for God's.

The "worship" here, Ellicott notes, is named by a word that "expresses... inward devotion" (as in Acts 18:13), "not... the outward act of homage" — so that even the inward religion of the Pharisees is pronounced empty. It is "in vain," Gill observes, because it rests not on God's appointment but on human invention; God is neither pleased by it nor are the worshippers profited.

The reason for its vanity is the second clause: "teaching for doctrines the commandments of men." Barnes explains they were "imposing your traditions as equal in authority to the commands of God" — pressing human precepts "as commands of God binding on the conscience." Ellicott distinguishes the Greek terms used through the passage: the word here "points to many detailed precepts," the one following to "the commandment which is exceeding broad" — the contrast between a multitude of human rules and the single, deep law of God.

Henry's tradition presses the indictment home. The Pharisees, "when they should have been pressing upon people the great principles of religion, were enforcing the canons of their church, and judged of people's being Jews or no according as they did or did not conform to them, without any consideration... whether they lived in obedience to God's laws." This, the witnesses agree, is the perennial peril of all merely traditional religion: to teach as divine what is only human, and so to make zeal for men's commandments a substitute for obedience to God's. The worship that should ascend to heaven is emptied of value the moment its rule is drawn from earth; for God will be served on his own terms, not on those that men devise and then ascribe to him. Gill notes the clause continues the citation from Isaiah, so that the prophet's ancient rebuke of will-worship falls with full force on the scribes; what was true of Israel in his day is true of every age that prizes its own inventions above the plain word of God.
8:
"For laying aside the commandment of God, ye hold the tradition of men, as the washing of pots and cups: and many other such like things ye do." The witnesses dwell on the deliberate setting-aside of God's law and the triviality preferred to it.

"Laying aside," Barnes explains, means "rejecting, or making it give place to traditions; considering the traditions as superior in authority to the Divine law" — "the uniform doctrine of the Pharisees." Robertson sharpens "the sharp contrast between the command of God and the traditions of men": "they had covered up the Word of God with their oral teaching," caring "more for the oral teaching of the scribes and elders than for the written law of God" — an indictment, he adds, that "the Talmud gives abundant and specific confirmation of."

The example named — "the washing of pots and cups" — exposes the disproportion. The commandment of God is set aside, while scrupulous care is lavished on the rinsing of vessels. Henry's tradition had already warned that "superstition is an endless thing": once one human rule is admitted, "many other such like things" follow, and the energy owed to God's weighty commands is spent on a multitude of trifles.

The witnesses note a textual point: the clause "as the washing of pots and cups" is absent from several of the better manuscripts (Clarke, Ellicott), perhaps assimilated from the earlier verses, though, as Ellicott observes, it is "hardly likely that such words should have been added as a note." Whether retained or not, the charge stands: the Pharisees had inverted the true order of religion, treating God's command as negotiable and their own tradition as binding. The witnesses find here the essential sin of traditionalism — not the having of customs, but the preferring of them to the revealed will of God, so that the lesser crowds out the greater and the human displaces the divine. Gill notes the "commandment of God" here means not one precept but "all the commandments of God, the whole written law," to which they preferred "the oral law... and the decisions of their doctors" — the entire revealed will of God subordinated to the rulings of men.
9:
"And he said unto them, Full well ye reject the commandment of God, that ye may keep your own tradition." The witnesses dwell on the biting irony and the force of the word "reject."

"Full well" (kalōs) the commentators take as scathing sarcasm. Barnes offers the readings — a question, or "skilfully, cunningly," or "ironical": "How nobly you act! From conscientious attachment to your traditions, you have made void the law of God." Clarke calls it "a strong irony — How noble is your conduct! From conscientious attachment to your own traditions ye have annihilated the commandments of God." Ellicott hears "the ring of a scathing and indignant irony," and Robertson "biting sarcasm that cut to the bone," so that "one can almost see the scribes withering under this terrible arraignment."

The word "reject" the witnesses find stronger than its English. It conveys, says Ellicott, "the idea... of rescinding or repealing" (as in Galatians 3:15), which "the Pharisees practically did when they added traditions which pretended to be interpretations, but were in reality at variance" with the law. Robertson notes the contrast with the milder word "set aside" in the previous verse: here it is a deliberate annulling "that ye may keep your traditions."

The commentators underline that the irony must not be mistaken for praise. Robertson warns against any "literal interpretation as commendation of the Pharisaic perversion of God's word." Clarke supplies a rabbinic confession that proves the charge: "Rabba said, How foolish are most men! They observe the precepts of the Divine law, and neglect the statutes of the rabbins!" — the very inversion Christ condemns, stated openly by the rabbis themselves. The witnesses thus see in the verse a deliberate exposure: by their own admission the scribes ranked the statutes of men above the law of God, and Christ, with cutting courtesy, lets the absurdity of that preference stand naked before them. Robertson notes the construction makes the keeping of their tradition the very purpose of the rejecting — "that ye may keep your traditions" — so that the abolishing of God's command was not an accident of their zeal but its deliberate aim.
10:
"For Moses said, Honour thy father and thy mother; and, Whoso curseth father or mother, let him die the death." The witnesses dwell on the command cited, its breadth, and its sanction.

The command is God's, given through Moses. Gill stresses it "was spoken by God, and written by him on one of the tables of stone." The two clauses are from Exodus 20:12 (also Deuteronomy 5:16) and Exodus 21:17 (also Leviticus 20:9) — the honour due to parents and the death decreed for cursing them.

The "honour," the fathers note, is more than words. Bede had observed (on v. 1) that "the sense of the word honour in Scripture is not so much the saluting and paying court to men, as alms-giving, and bestowing gifts" — citing "honour widows that are widows indeed." Henry's tradition draws the inference: "it is the duty of children, if their parents be poor, to relieve them, according to their ability"; and "if those children are worthy to die that curse their parents, much more those that starve them." The command thus reaches to the maintenance of needy parents, not mere outward respect.

The sanction is severe — "let him die the death." Christ sets this divine law, with its threat, over against the scribes' device, to show how grave their evasion was. Cyril of Jerusalem balances the duty rightly: "while honouring our heavenly Father, let us honour also the fathers of our flesh," for "children, obey your parents in all things" (cf. Ephesians 6:1); yet "when our fathers on earth are of a contrary mind to our Father in heaven, then we must obey Christ's word." Where parents "put no obstacle to godliness," to hold them "in contempt" brings down "the oracle which says, He that curseth father or mother, let him die." The witnesses thus establish the weight of the command the Pharisees had circumvented: a law of God, rooted in nature, enforced by the highest penalty, and binding children to the active care of those who bore them. Gill stresses that the precept was no mere human counsel but was "written by God on one of the tables of stone, and delivered into the hands of Moses," so that to evade it was to despise not the word of a man but the express finger of God.
11:
"But ye say, If a man shall say to his father or mother, It is Corban, that is to say, a gift, by whatsoever thou mightest be profited by me; he shall be free." The witnesses explain the word Corban and the evasion it enabled.

"But ye say" sets the scribes squarely against Moses. The device turned on the word Corban, which Mark, writing for Gentiles, preserves in Hebrew. It means "a gift" or "offering" (Robertson, Ellicott), a word frequent in Leviticus and Numbers (Leviticus 2:1; Numbers 7:3), and "had come to be applied specifically... to the sacred treasure of the Temple" (Ellicott; cf. Matthew 27:6). Lightfoot confirms from the rabbis that "the word 'a gift' was known and common among the Talmudists."

The trick lay in the vow. By declaring his property "Corban" — devoted to God — a son could plead himself "free" from supporting his parents. Robertson states the scandal plainly: "the rabbis actually allowed the mere saying of this word by an unfaithful son to prevent the use of needed money for the support of father or mother"; worse, "they held that he was prohibited from using it for father or mother, but he might use it for himself." It was, he says, "a home thrust to these pettifogging sticklers for ceremonial punctilios."

Theophylact had exposed the motive (on v. 1): "the Pharisees, wishing to devour the offerings, instructed sons" so to answer their parents, "thus deceiving the sons into neglecting their parents, whilst they themselves devoured the offerings." Ellicott adds a textual note: "he shall be free" has no exact equivalent in the Greek, the sense running on into the next verse — "if a man shall say... ye suffer him no more." The witnesses thus lay bare the heart of the abuse: a pious-sounding formula, invoking the claims of God, became the cloak under which a man might rob his own parents and the scribes might enrich the Temple treasury — religion turned into an instrument of greed and unnatural cruelty. Lightfoot illustrates from the rabbis how freely the word was used — "a burnt sacrifice is a gift," they said, and even spoke of Egypt bringing "a gift" to the Messiah — showing how familiar and how easily abused the formula of devotion had become.
12:
"And ye suffer him no more to do ought for his father or his mother." The witnesses dwell on the cruelty of the device and the human authority it set above God.

Gill expounds the rabbinic canon at length. "If a man vowed a thing which is contrary to a command, he was obliged to keep his vow, and break the command." So if a son "vowed that his father or his mother should never receive any benefit from what he had... he was obliged to keep his vow; nor was he allowed after this to do any thing for his father or mother, however poor or helpless they might be." He could not annul it himself, "as wicked as it was, and though he might heartily repent of it"; only "a wise man" could revoke it. And though such dissolution was allowed, Gill notes, "hereby they set up their own power and authority against God and his law" — for "they did not rescind the vow because it was contrary to the command of God," but by the authority of their own doctors.

The fathers had named the same evil. Pseudo-Chrysostom (on v. 1) charged that "in saying this, they did not allow parents to be honoured"; Theophylact, that they reproached God's law "for the sake of gain." Henry's tradition draws the monstrous conclusion: a man who would not relieve his needy parents had only to "swear by the Corban... that his parents shall not be profited by him," and "it is enough; as if by the obligation of this wicked vow he had discharged himself from the obligation of God's holy law."

The witnesses thus find the verse the clinching proof of Christ's charge. The tradition did not merely add to God's command; it forbade obedience to it. The son who wished to evade his duty was not only permitted but "suffered no more" to honour his parents — bound by a vow the elders held more sacred than the fifth commandment. Henry's tradition sees in it the very pattern of later corruptions, "discharging children from all obligation to their parents by their monastic vows." Where human authority is allowed to bind what God has loosed and loose what God has bound, the witnesses observe, the result is not piety but the sanctifying of plain wickedness.
13:
"Making the word of God of none effect through your tradition, which ye have delivered: and many such like things do ye." The witnesses dwell on the force of the charge and its breadth.

"Making of none effect," the grammatical commentators note, is a strong word — "making null and void, cancelling" (Ellicott; cf. Galatians 3:17); "invalidating," says Robertson, "a stronger word than to set aside in verse 9," for "setting aside does invalidate." The instrument of the cancelling is "your tradition" — the structure pointing, Ellicott observes, to the tradition "as being the instrument with which the Law was made null and void." It is, Gill notes, a tradition the scribes themselves "have delivered," resting on no authority but their own.

Gill draws out the folly Christ exposes (some copies, he notes, read "your foolish tradition"): "that a vow made rashly, and in a passion... should be more binding upon a man than the law of God; that rather than break this, he should transgress a divine command." Should his distressed parents appeal to "the command of God, to honour them," the son was "instructed" to answer that "it was the sense of the wise men and doctors... to which he ought rather to attend." Theophylact (on v. 1) had summed the indictment: the scribes were "transgressing the law of God for the sake of gain."

The closing words, "many such like things do ye," the witnesses take as an emphatic widening of the charge. The Corban evasion was no isolated case but a specimen; Ellicott hears "an emphatic scorn... in this iteration of the same formula" used earlier (v. 8). The whole system of tradition, Christ declares, was riddled with such nullifyings of God's word. The witnesses thus close the argument where Henry's tradition had begun it: "where will men stop, when once they have made the word of God give way to their tradition?" The Pharisees had begun by making light of God's commandments in comparison with their own, and ended by making them void — a warning, the commentators agree, against every religion that exalts the precepts of men to the place of the law of God.
14:
"And when he had called all the people unto him, he said unto them, Hearken unto me every one of you, and understand." The witnesses dwell on the turn to the crowd and the principle Christ now lays down.

Having silenced the scribes, Christ addresses the people. Gill notes he "turns himself to the common people, who stood at some distance," and "called to them to come nearer." Robertson reads it as "a most pointed appeal to the people to see into and see through the chicanery of these ecclesiastics" — Jesus "turned the tables on them completely and laid bare their hollow pretentious hypocrisy." Henry's tradition explains why he taught this publicly: the lesson "related to daily practice," and "corrupt customs are best cured by rectifying corrupt notions"; so he bids them not only "hear, but understand what they hear" (cf. Acts 8:30).

The principle overturns the whole theory behind the hand-washing: defilement comes not from without but from within. Pseudo-Chrysostom frames the contrast: "the things of Christ have relation to the inner man, but those which are of the law are visible and external," and these external observances, "as being bodily, the cross of Christ was shortly to put an end" to. Bede draws the rule: "according to the state of his heart a man should be called clean or unclean," so that "whatsoever does not reach the soul cannot bring pollution to the man."

The fathers also guard against a false inference. Bede warns that "from this passage are condemned those men who suppose that thoughts are put into them by the devil, and do not arise from their own evil will": "the devil may excite and help on evil thoughts, he cannot be their author." Theophylact adds that "whatsoever is in the power of a man is imputed to him as a fault, because all such things proceed from the interior will, by which man is master of his own actions." The witnesses thus set the keynote of the whole discourse: true purity and pollution are matters of the heart, and the cleansing the gospel requires is inward, not ceremonial (cf. Matthew 15:11).
15:
"There is nothing from without a man, that entering into him can defile him: but the things which come out of him, those are they that defile the man." The witnesses dwell on the sweeping principle and its careful limits.

Gill states the plain sense: "there is nothing from without a man — as any sort of food and drink, whether it be received with or without washing of the hands" — that can pollute the soul. The defilement that matters proceeds outward, "from within," not inward from the table. Bede had grounded this (on v. 14): meats "since they do not reach the soul, cannot in their own nature defile a man."

Wesley adds a needful qualification. "It is very true," he allows, "a man may bring guilt, which is moral defilement, upon himself by eating what hurts his health, or by excess either in meat or drink" — gluttony and drunkenness are real sins. "Yet even here the pollution arises from the wickedness of the heart, and is just proportionable to it. And this is all that our Lord asserts." The food itself is innocent; the sin lies in the disordered will that abuses it. Bede had said the same: "an inordinate use of meats, which proceeds from a want of order in the mind, makes men unclean."

The witnesses thus find in the saying a quiet revolution. The whole edifice of ceremonial purity — clean and unclean foods, washed and unwashed hands — is shown to touch only the outward man, while the real defilement, the only kind that separates from God, springs from the heart. Pseudo-Chrysostom had noted that "our Lord wishes to bring in the contrary" of the Jewish bodily purification. The principle does not licence excess, for excess too is a sin of the heart; but it removes religion from the surface to the centre, teaching that a man is made clean or unclean not by what he handles or eats, but by what he is and what proceeds from him. Pseudo-Chrysostom adds that "the things of Christ have relation to the inner man, but those which are of the law are visible and external" — so that this saying quietly announces the passing of the bodily purifications to which the cross would soon put an end.
16:
"If any man have ears to hear, let him hear." The witnesses treat the familiar formula, its effect on the disciples, and its textual standing.

The saying is Christ's customary call to attention. Ellicott notes "it was with this formula that our Lord had closed some at least of His parables" (Mark 4:9; Matthew 13:9), and that "it was probably this that led to the form which the inquiry of the disciples took when they came to ask their Master concerning the parable." Barnes elsewhere glosses the phrase as a summons to spiritual attention (cf. Matthew 11:15): the words are weighty, and demand more than a careless hearing.

The call implies that the truth just spoken was both important and easily missed. Pseudo-Chrysostom had observed (on v. 14) that Christ "had not clearly shewn them what those things are which... defile a man," and that "on account of this saying, the Apostles thought that the foregoing discourse... implied some other deep thing." The formula thus marks the principle of inward defilement as a deep saying, to be pondered, not passed over — one that the natural mind, schooled in ceremonial religion, would be slow to receive.

The witnesses note a textual point: Ellicott observes that "the whole verse is... omitted in many of the best MSS., and may have been originally a marginal note written by some early transcriber to call attention to the truth stated in the text." Whether spoken here or borrowed from its frequent use elsewhere, the exhortation fits the moment. Cyril of Jerusalem reads it of the spiritual faculty God gives: in baptism the ears are anointed "that ye might receive the ears which are quick to hear the Divine Mysteries," fulfilling the Lord's own word, "He that hath ears to hear, let him hear." The witnesses thus take the verse as a pointer: the teaching on what truly defiles is no surface matter, but a mystery requiring the opened ear of faith to receive. The same Lord who spoke parables to veil the truth from the careless here invites the willing to press past the figure to the substance; the formula is at once a warning against inattention and a promise that those who truly listen will be given to understand.
17:
"And when he was entered into the house from the people, his disciples asked him concerning the parable." The witnesses note the private setting and the disciples' difficulty.

The house, Robertson suggests, was "probably Peter's house in Capernaum," where Jesus often lodged; Gill thinks the same, "the house of Simon and Andrew." The withdrawal indoors marks a familiar pattern: "to the crowd Jesus spoke the parable," but "the disciples want it interpreted" (Robertson; cf. Mark 4:10, where they likewise ask privately). Matthew, Robertson adds, "represents Peter as the spokesman, as was usually the case" (Matthew 15:15).

That they called it "the parable" shows it was dark to them. Pseudo-Chrysostom had noted (on v. 14) "they called it parable, because it was not clear." Barnes explains the cause of their perplexity: "they had probably imbibed many of the popular notions of the Pharisees, and they could not understand why a man was not defiled by external things"; moreover "it was a doctrine of the law that men were ceremonially polluted by contact with dead bodies," and the like, "and they could not understand how it could be otherwise."

The witnesses thus find in the disciples' question both honesty and dullness. Unlike the Pharisees, who took offence and walked away, the disciples bring their difficulty to their Master, seeking light. Yet the difficulty itself reveals how deeply the categories of clean and unclean were ingrained in them — so ingrained that a single saying overturning the whole system struck them as a riddle. The scene is characteristic of Mark's Gospel: the crowd hears the truth in figure, and the disciples, drawn apart with Christ, receive its plain interpretation — though even they must first confess they do not understand. It is to teachable but slow learners, the commentators observe, that the Lord unfolds his meaning, rewarding the question that the Pharisees were too proud to ask. Robertson notes the detail of the entry into the house "from the multitude" is Mark's own, a glimpse of the quiet circle where the Twelve, drawn apart, could bring their perplexities to the Master and have the riddle of the parable made plain.
18:
"And he saith unto them, Are ye so without understanding also? Do ye not perceive, that whatsoever thing from without entereth into the man, it cannot defile him?" The witnesses dwell on the gentle rebuke and the truth restated.

The question carries a note of disappointment. Gill reads it as spoken "with some warmth of spirit and resentment, at their stupidity." Robertson calls it "a discouraging moment for the great Teacher if his own chosen pupils were still under the spell of the Pharisaic theological outlook." The "also" stings: "you also, as well as the multitude" — even the disciples are dull. "They had been trained in Judaism," Robertson notes (with Gould), "in which the distinction between clean and unclean is ingrained, and could not understand a statement abrogating this." They had seen the Pharisees stumble at the saying (cf. Matt 15:12), and were "stumbling themselves, and did not know how to answer." Theophylact had likewise observed that "the Lord begins by chiding them."

The truth itself Christ restates plainly: "whatsoever thing from without entereth into the man, it cannot defile him." Barnes glosses "cannot defile him" as "cannot render his soul polluted; cannot make him a sinner, so as to need this purifying as a religious observance." The point is not merely that external things are harmless to the body, but that they cannot touch the moral state of the soul, and therefore cannot create the kind of impurity that religion exists to cleanse.

The witnesses find the rebuke instructive as well as severe. Christ does not abandon his dull pupils but presses the lesson, charging them, as Robertson says, "with intellectual dulness and spiritual stupidity" precisely in order to lift them out of it. The chiding is the discipline of a teacher who expects more of those he has chosen, and who will not let them rest in the inherited errors of the very system he had come to overturn. To perceive that no outward thing can defile the soul was, for men reared on the law of clean and unclean, a hard but necessary advance into the spiritual understanding of God's will.
19:
"Because it entereth not into his heart, but into the belly, and goeth out into the draught, purging all meats." The witnesses dwell on the reason given and on Mark's momentous closing comment.

The reason is that food never reaches the soul. "It entereth not into his heart," Gill explains — "which is the seat and fountain of all moral pollution" — but only "into the belly." The "heart," Ellicott notes, is "after the common Hebrew idiom, the symbol of the mind as well as the affections" (cf. Proverbs 9:4). Barnes traces the physiology with some care: the food taken in is digested, "the unwholesome parts are separated, and the wholesome only are taken into the system" — so that what passes through "cannot defile the soul" (cf. 1 Corinthians 6:13).

The closing clause, "purging all meats," the grammatical commentators treat as a famous crux. The better manuscripts give the participle in the masculine, which cannot agree with "draught." Ellicott and Robertson therefore take it as Mark's own comment, agreeing with the subject of "he saith" (v. 18): "in so saying, Jesus cleansed all meats" — or, as Robertson supplies it, "making all meats clean." "The words do not come from Jesus," Robertson notes, "but are added by Mark," who had it "from Peter, probably with a vivid recollection of his own experience on the housetop in Joppa," where "what God cleansed do not thou treat as defiled" (Acts 10:15; cf. Acts 10:14).

The witnesses thus find here a declaration of the first magnitude. Ellicott calls it anticipating "in almost the same terms the truth of Acts 10:15." It was, Robertson says, "a revolutionary declaration by Jesus," that "uncleanness of heart... is of vast importance compared with" ceremonial defilement — "and Peter was slow to understand it even after... Pentecost." In a single parenthetical phrase, the Evangelist records that the Lord, in teaching that nothing entering defiles, had abolished the whole Mosaic distinction of clean and unclean foods — opening the way, though the apostles scarcely yet perceived it, for the free fellowship of Jew and Gentile at one table.
20:
"And he said, That which cometh out of the man, that defileth the man." The witnesses dwell on the positive counterpart to the principle: the true source of pollution.

Having shown what cannot defile, Christ now names what does. Gill notes the words "continued... in his discourse." Barnes explains "that which cometh out of the man" as "his words; the expression of his thoughts and feelings; his conduct, as the expression of inward malice, anger, covetousness, lust" — and these "defileth the man," that is, "render the soul corrupt and abominable in the sight of God."

The image the witnesses draw is of a fountain and its streams. Henry's tradition (on the chapter) puts it: "as a corrupt fountain sends forth corrupt streams, so doth a corrupt heart send forth corrupt reasonings, corrupt appetites and passions, and all those wicked words and actions which are produced by them." The Gloss had located the source precisely (on v. 14): "evil thoughts belong to the mind, which is here called the heart, and according to which a man is called good or bad, clean or unclean."

The witnesses thus see the verse completing the reversal Christ has been working. The Pharisees feared defilement from without — from unwashed hands, from contact with the unclean — and guarded against it with endless washings. Christ teaches that the real defilement wells up from within, from the heart, and shows itself in word and deed. The cleansing that matters, therefore, cannot be ceremonial; it must reach the fountain. Henry's tradition draws the practical conclusion: "our care must therefore be to wash our heart from wickedness." The verse stands as the hinge of the discourse, turning the hearer's attention away from the outward observances that occupied the scribes and fixing it on the inward purity that God requires — the purity, or pollution, of the heart itself (cf. Matthew 15:18). Gill notes that the words are a deliberate resumption of the discourse — "he said," continuing to press the point — so that the lesson should not be lost: it is not the meat on the table but the man at the table whose state determines whether he is clean or unclean before God.
21:
"For from within, out of the heart of men, proceed evil thoughts, fornications, thefts, murders." The witnesses dwell on the corrupt fountain and the catalogue of sins that flow from it.

The heart is the source of all. Gill paints it darkly: "the inside of man is very bad, his inward part is not only wicked, but wickedness itself"; "in him dwells no good thing naturally, his heart is wicked, and desperately so" (cf. Jeremiah 17:9; Genesis 6:5). "All its powers and faculties are vitiated" — understanding, conscience, affections alike — so that "what good thing... can come out of such a Nazareth as this? Nothing, but" the evils now listed.

"Evil thoughts" stand first, the witnesses note, as the spring of the rest. Henry's tradition observes that "ill-thinking is put first, as that which is the spring of all our commissions"; the Gloss (on v. 14) likewise: "from evil thoughts... evil actions proceed to greater lengths." Robertson surveys the catalogue as "the crimes of a modern police court," coming "out of the heart, the inner man" — "fornications" (of the unmarried), "thefts" (stealings), "murders" (often growing out of the others).

The order, the commentators observe, is significant. The list moves from the inward fountain — evil thoughts — to the outward acts they breed. As a poisoned spring cannot but send forth poisoned water, so the corrupt heart cannot but issue in corrupt deeds; the sins named are not accidents imposed from outside but the native produce of the soil within. This is the decisive answer to the Pharisees' anxiety about external defilement: the danger lies not in what a man touches or eats but in what he is. The witnesses thus find the verse a sober anatomy of human depravity, tracing the whole train of visible wickedness back to its hidden source in a heart that, untouched by grace, is itself "very wickedness." Gill presses that "out of the abundance of it proceed the evil things hereafter mentioned," and that "every imagination of the thought of the heart is evil, and that continually" — so that the catalogue is not a list of occasional lapses but a portrait of the natural heart's constant output.
22:
"Thefts, covetousness, wickedness, deceit, lasciviousness, an evil eye, blasphemy, pride, foolishness." The witnesses dwell on the vices that complete the dreadful catalogue, several of which Mark alone records.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the terms. "Covetousness" and "wickedness" are plural, Ellicott notes, "pointing to the manifold forms in which the sins show themselves"; "covetousness," Robertson adds, is "the craze for more and more." "Wickedness" (ponēria) Wesley takes as "ill-nature, cruelty, inhumanity, and all malevolent affections," and Robertson as the disposition of one grown criminal. "Deceit" is "the lure or snare with bait" (Robertson); "lasciviousness," "unrestrained sex instinct," "any corruption of mind or body" (Gloss).

Three of the closing vices the commentators treat with care. "An evil eye," Ellicott explains (citing Matthew 20:15), is "envy in its most malignant form," that "looks askance on the good of others." "Pride," better "haughtiness" — a word, Ellicott notes, found only here in this form in the New Testament (the adjective in Romans 1:30) — is, Robertson says, "holding oneself above others, stuck up." "Foolishness," another rare word, is, as Proverbs 14:18 interprets it, "the folly which consists in the absence of the fear of God, the infatuation of impiety" (Ellicott); Henry's tradition takes it of "that rashness in speaking and acting which is the cause of so much evil."

The witnesses note Mark's distinctive list. He "omits false witness," which Matthew has, Gill observes, "and adds the following," several "not taken notice of by the other evangelists." Henry's tradition reads a deliberate order: "ill-thinking is put first... and unthinking put last, as that which is the spring of all our omissions" — the catalogue running from the corrupt thought through the whole range of corrupt desire, speech, and act, and closing fittingly with the foolishness that is impiety's root. The sheer length and variety of the list, the commentators observe, is itself an argument: so many and so various are the streams that the fountain from which they all flow must be deeply and pervasively corrupt. Robertson calls the closing "foolishness" (aphrosynē, lack of sense) "a fitting close to it all" — the impiety that, having cast off the fear of God, is left without the wisdom that alone could restrain the whole train of evils that go before.
23:
"All these evil things come from within, and defile the man." The witnesses dwell on the conclusion: the inward origin of sin and the doctrine of human corruption it implies.

Christ gathers the whole list to its source. Gill draws out the inference: "all evil thoughts, words, and actions take their rise from the inward parts of man, from his heart, which is sadly corrupted, and is the fountain from whence all these impure streams flow." And "if these come from within, then not from without; they are not by imitation, or are the mere effects of example in others." Example, he allows, "may indeed, and often does, draw out the evil that is within; but it does not produce it there; if it was not there before, it could not draw it out."

From this Gill reasons to original sin. "If all these evils come from within, then the inward part of man must be sinful and polluted, previous to the commission of these evil things." Whence then is this inward pollution? "It is the fruit of original sin, of Adam's transgression; the consequence of which is a corrupt nature, which is derived to all his posterity" — for, he asks with Scripture, "who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean?" (Job 14:4). The evils that defile are not learned from without but inherited within.

The closing word, "and defile the man," the witnesses take in its full weight. To defile, Henry's tradition explains, is to "render a man unfit for communion with God," to "bring a stain upon the conscience"; and if such sins are "not mortified and rooted out, [they] will shut men out of the new Jerusalem, into which no unclean thing shall enter." The verse thus seals the discourse: the Pharisees' whole system of outward washing was directed at a pollution that does not exist where they feared it, and blind to the pollution that truly damns. The cleansing man needs is not of the hands but of the heart — a cleansing, the witnesses imply, that no tradition of the elders could supply, but only the grace of the Saviour who taught it.
24:
"And from thence he arose, and went into the borders of Tyre and Sidon, and entered into an house, and would have no man know it: but he could not be hid." The witnesses dwell on the withdrawal into Gentile country, the desire for privacy, and the puzzle of his "could not."

The move is into heathen land. Theophylact reads it as a turning: "the Jews being unfaithful, salvation turns itself to the Gentiles." Robertson calls it the second withdrawal from Galilee — "too much excitement among the people, too much bitterness among the Pharisees, too much suspicion on the part of Herod... for Jesus to remain"; he "craved a little privacy and rest" (cf. Matthew 15:21). Henry's tradition adds that, "to teach us not to be fond of popular applause," he sought a private house where "he would have no man know it."

The clause "he could not be hid" the fathers handle with care. Pseudo-Augustine asks how, if "he wished to do so and could not, it appears as if his will was impotent"; he answers that "the time had not come to preach" to the Gentiles, yet "not to receive them when they came... of their own accord would have been to grudge them the faith." So Christ "willed that he should not be proclaimed by his own disciples, but that others should come to seek him, and so it took place." Calvin, speaking "according to the ordinary perception of the flesh," says that as "the minister and ambassador of the Father" he "kept himself... within the limits of that calling," so that "what he wished, he was unable to accomplish."

The episode, Calvin observes, "tends powerfully to condemn the Jews": they who "boasted that they were the heirs of the covenant" were "blind and deaf when Christ... offered to them the promised redemption," while "this woman, who had no relationship with the children of Abraham... came of her own accord to Christ." Pseudo-Jerome reads it mystically: "the Gentile woman who prays for her daughter is our mother the Church" of the nations, her daughter "the barbarian western race, which by faith hath been turned from a dog into a sheep." The withdrawal thus becomes, in the witnesses' reading, the first quiet opening of the door of faith to the Gentiles.
25:
"For a certain woman, whose young daughter had an unclean spirit, heard of him, and came and fell at his feet." The witnesses dwell on the woman's distress, the reach of Christ's fame, and the humility of her approach.

Even in heathen country his name was known. Robertson notes "even in this heathen territory the fame of Jesus was known"; when the Sermon on the Mount was preached, people were present "from the sea coast of Tyre and Sidon" (Luke 6:17). So this woman, "having heard of him," came at once. Robertson marks the tender diminutive — "whose little daughter" — and the vernacular ring of "whose" and "her."

Her trouble was grievous. Henry's tradition reflects on it: "how many and grievous are the calamities that young children are subject to!" The daughter "had an unclean spirit," a possession beyond any human help, which drove the mother to the only one who could deliver her.

Her approach was lowly. "She heard of him, and came, and fell at his feet," and in this, Henry's tradition finds the pattern of all true suppliants: "those that would obtain mercy from Christ must throw themselves at his feet; must refer themselves to him, humble themselves before him, and give up themselves to be ruled by him." And there is encouragement in it: "Christ never put any from him that fell at his feet, which a poor trembling soul may do, that has not boldness and confidence to throw itself into his arms." The witnesses thus see in this Gentile mother, prostrate before a Jewish stranger of whom she had only heard, a model of the faith that, knowing its need and his power, casts itself wholly upon his mercy — and a foretaste of the multitude of the nations who would come after her to fall at the same feet. Robertson notes the diminutive "little daughter" (thugatrion) carries "a tender touch," and that even in this heathen region "the fame of Jesus was known"; the mother had only report to go on, yet report was enough to bring her, believing, to the feet of one she had never seen.
26:
"The woman was a Greek, a Syrophenician by nation; and she besought him that he would cast forth the devil out of her daughter." The witnesses dwell on the woman's origin and the persistence of her plea.

"A Greek" here means a Gentile. Barnes explains "the Jews called all persons Greeks who were not of their nation," the world being "divided into Jews and Greeks" (cf. Romans 1:16); Ellicott compares the modern Eastern use of "Frank" for any European. Her nation is given precisely: "Syrophenician," a word, Ellicott notes, that is "an instance of St. Mark's tendency to use Latin forms" (it appears in Juvenal), reflecting the Roman division of Syria. Robertson sums the description from Euthymius: "a Greek in religion, a Syrian in tongue, a Phoenician in race." Gill observes that her being a Gentile "made her faith the more remarkable."

Her petition was urgent and sustained. "She besought him," Robertson notes, is an imperfect — "she kept at it" — the verb used "for a request, not a mere question." Henry's tradition marks how "particular" it was: "she tells him what she wanted... that he would cast forth the devil out of her daughter," for "Christ gave poor supplicants leave to be thus free with him."

The witnesses draw the lesson of the request itself. Henry's tradition reads in it the highest thing a parent can ask: "the greatest blessing we can ask of Christ for our children is that he would break the power of Satan, that is, the power of sin, in their souls; and particularly that he would cast forth the unclean spirit, that they may be temples of the Holy Ghost." This heathen mother, with no claim on the covenant and no acquaintance with the law, knew enough to bring her child's deepest need to the one who could meet it — and to keep on asking until she was heard. Her foreign blood, far from disqualifying her, only set her faith in brighter relief against the unbelief of Israel. Ellicott likens the Jewish use of "Greek" for any Gentile to the modern Eastern use of "Frank" for any European, a name whose original sense has widened; and Wesley notes that Canaan was called Syrophenicia "as lying between Syria, properly so called, and Phenicia."
27:
"But Jesus said unto her, Let the children first be filled: for it is not meet to take the children's bread, and to cast it unto the dogs." The witnesses dwell on the apparent refusal, the Jews' priority, and the trial of faith.

The "children" are the Jews, the "dogs" the Gentiles. The Jews, Robertson notes, "had the first claim" (cf. Matthew 10:5; Romans 2:9, "to the Jew first"), and Christ calls the Gentiles "dogs, as being thought wicked by the Jews" (Theophylact). Pseudo-Chrysostom insists the words spring "not that there is in him a deficiency of virtue... but because his benefit, if ministered to both Jews and Gentiles who had no communication with each other, might be a cause of jealousy" — and that "by the discovery of the woman's faith, the unfaithfulness of the Jews might be the more laid bare."

The word "first" the witnesses find full of hope. Ellicott notes the form of the answer is peculiar to Mark; and Henry's tradition seizes on "let the children first be filled" as intimating "that there was mercy in reserve for the Gentiles, and not far off"; "the children begin to play with their meat, and their leavings... would be a feast for the Gentiles." The apostles, he adds, "went by this rule, Let the children first be filled, let the Jews have the first offer."

Above all, the saying was a trial of faith. Theophylact explains that "the reason why the Lord does not immediately hear, but delays his grace, is that he may shew that the faith of the woman was firm, and that we may learn not at once to grow weary in prayer, but to continue earnest till we obtain." Henry's tradition agrees: "where Christ knows the faith of poor supplicants to be strong, he sometimes delights to try it, and put it to the stretch." The harsh-sounding word, the witnesses conclude, was no rejection but a test — drawing out the very faith it seemed to repel, and proving the woman worthy, in the end, of the blessing reserved beyond the children's table.
28:
"And she answered and said unto him, Yes, Lord: yet the dogs under the table eat of the children's crumbs." The witnesses delight in the woman's humble, witty, and faith-filled reply.

She accepts the lowly name and turns it to her plea. Pseudo-Chrysostom marvels (on v. 24) that "her placing herself in the rank of dogs is a mark of her reverence; as if she said, I hold it as a favour to be even in the position of a dog, and to eat... from the table of the Master himself." Robertson calls it "a unique combination of faith and wit": "instead of resenting Christ's words about giving the children's bread to the dogs, she instantly turned it to the advantage of her plea." Henry's tradition paraphrases: "I ask not for a loaf, no, nor for a morsel, only for a crumb; do not refuse me that."

The diminutives heighten the tenderness of the picture. Robertson notes the "little dogs" (kynaria) under the table, the "little scraps" (psichia) of bread, the "little children" who, "purposely," perhaps, "dropped a few little crumbs for the little dogs" — household pets "loved by the children." Theophylact had given her sense (on v. 24): "the Jews have the whole of that bread which comes down from heaven... I ask for the crumbs, that is, a small portion of the benefit."

The witnesses find in her answer the very triumph of faith. She does not dispute the order Christ has named — "Yes, Lord" — but finds within it room for her hope: if the children are served first, the dogs are not therefore sent away, for even they have their crumbs. Henry's tradition reads her as "magnifying the abundance of miraculous cures... in comparison with which a single cure was but as a crumb." So the woman makes Christ's own word her ground of appeal, and her humility becomes her boldness: claiming nothing as of right, she claims everything as of grace, and asks only for the overflow of a table so richly spread that even its scraps would heal her child.
29:
"And he said unto her, For this saying go thy way; the devil is gone out of thy daughter." The witnesses dwell on the ground of the grant and the granting itself.

The cure is given "for this saying" — that is, for the faith it revealed. Theophylact (on v. 24) is careful: Christ "said not, My virtue hath made thee whole, but for this saying, that is, for thy faith, which is shewn by this saying." Robertson notes that, though "she had faith, great faith as Matthew 15:28 shows, it was her quick and bright repartee that pleased Jesus": "he had missed his rest, but it was worth it to answer a call like this." Ellicott observes that Mark omits the words "O woman, great is thy faith," and "puts the answer to the prayer in a somewhat more definite form" than Matthew's "be it unto thee even as thou wilt."

The granting was immediate and complete. The word "the devil is gone out of thy daughter" did the thing it announced — "Christ's saying that it was done," Henry's tradition observes, "did it effectually, as at other times his saying, Let it be done." Gill notes some take "for this saying" of Christ's own powerful word, but rightly refers it to "the saying of the woman," her word of faith, while the cure follows in the words that come after.

The witnesses draw the encouragement. Henry's tradition makes the scene a spur to persevering prayer: it "encourages us to pray and not to faint, to continue instant in prayer, not doubting but to prevail at last." The woman who would not be turned away by silence, by the disciples' impatience, or by a word that seemed to shut the door, is sent home with her whole request granted — proof that importunate faith, however unlikely its bearer, is never finally denied. The Lord who tried her faith to the stretch crowns it at last, and her bright humility is rewarded with the deliverance of her child. Robertson adds that, though he "had missed his rest, it was worth it to answer a call like this" — the Saviour who sought solitude in a heathen house gladly broke it for the sake of one importunate Gentile mother and her tormented daughter.
30:
"And when she was come to her house, she found the devil gone out, and her daughter laid upon the bed." The witnesses dwell on the fulfilment of Christ's word and the power that wrought it at a distance.

She went home in faith and found it as he had said. Gill notes she "was abundantly satisfied" with Christ's words, "and went away with as great a faith, and as strong a persuasion of the dispossession, as that she came with"; "accordingly" she found it so. Henry's tradition adds that "she came to her house depending upon the word of Christ that her daughter was healed, and so she found it."

The scene is told with Mark's characteristic vividness. The daughter is "laid upon the bed" — Ellicott calls "the graphic description, as usual, characteristic of St. Mark." Henry's tradition pictures her "not in any toss or agitation, but very quietly laid on the bed, and reposing herself, waiting for her mother's return." Clarke records a notable Ethiopic reading — "she found her daughter clothed, sitting upon the couch, and the demon gone out" — and Robertson notes the perfect participle "gone out" (exelēluthos) "expresses the state of completion": the demon "was gone for good and all."

The witnesses fasten on the power exercised at a distance. Henry's tradition observes that "Christ can conquer Satan at a distance," and that "it was not only when the demoniacs saw him that they yielded to his power... but when they saw him not, for the Spirit of the Lord is not bound, nor bounded." The child, far off in her home, was delivered by a word spoken in the house where her mother knelt. So the story closes, the witnesses note, with a double triumph: of Christ's power, which reaches where his bodily presence is not, and of the woman's faith, which took him at his word and was not disappointed. This crumb from the children's table — a single Gentile child set free — was the firstfruits of the great harvest of the nations that her faith foreshadowed (cf. 1 John 3:8).
31:
"And again, departing from the coasts of Tyre and Sidon, he came unto the sea of Galilee, through the midst of the coasts of Decapolis." The witnesses trace the route, explain the return, and read the journey mystically.

The geography the commentators map carefully. Decapolis, Bede notes, "is a region of ten cities, across the Jordan, to the east, over against Galilee"; so when Christ is said to come "through the midst of the borders of Decapolis," it means he "came to the borders of the sea... opposite to the midst of the coasts of Decapolis," not that "he entered the confines... themselves." The movement is back from heathen territory toward the lake.

The reason for the return Theophylact supplies: "the Lord did not wish to stay in the parts of the Gentiles, lest he should give the Jews occasion to say that they esteemed him a transgressor of the law, because he held communion with the Gentiles; and therefore he immediately returns." The healing that follows, Theophylact adds, is "rightly placed after the deliverance of one possessed with a devil, for such an instance of suffering came from the devil."

Pseudo-Jerome reads the route as a parable of redemption. "Tyre is interpreted narrowness, and signifies Judaea," from which "the Lord turns himself to the Gentiles"; "Sidon means hunting, for our race is like an untamed beast"; the "sea" means "a wavering inconstancy"; and Decapolis "may be interpreted as the commands of the Decalogue." So "the human race throughout its many members is reckoned as one man, eaten up by varying pestilence," blind and deaf and dumb in the first-created Adam, now brought to Christ for healing. The witnesses thus see in the journey both the historical caution of the Saviour, careful to give the Jews no false occasion of offence, and a figure of his coming to heal the deaf and speechless race of men. Bede adds that, since a portion of Decapolis lay even on the western side of Jordan, the text may be taken quite literally; the Lord skirted the lake opposite that region of ten cities, returning from heathen ground to the borders of Israel.
32:
"And they bring unto him one that was deaf, and had an impediment in his speech; and they beseech him to put his hand upon him." The witnesses examine the man's condition and the manner of the appeal.

The man's affliction the commentators define with care. He was "deaf," and "had an impediment in his speech" — not wholly mute, but speaking "indistinctly or with difficulty" (Barnes). Clarke notes the Greek (mogilalos) "literally signifies one that cannot speak plainly, a stammerer," yet the word also means "a dumb person," and "it is generally found that he who is totally deaf is dumb also" — the deafness, very likely, the cause of the impediment. Ellicott observes that the same word "stands for dumb in the Greek version of Isaiah 35:6," and "may therefore have been used... to connect the miracle... with that prophecy."

The appeal was made by his friends. "They bring unto him" — "his friends brought, or the people brought" (Barnes) — and "beseech him to put his hand upon him," the laying on of hands being, Barnes notes, the customary way "blessings were commonly imparted." Calvin explains it was "a solemn symbol of consecration," by which "the gifts of the Holy Spirit were also bestowed," so that "those men requested nothing but what they knew that he had been formerly in the habit of doing." Robertson notes the incident "only in Mark," told with "another of Mark's dramatic presents."

The witnesses thus set the scene of a helpless sufferer and his believing friends. The man could neither hear the gracious words of Christ nor answer them; he was wholly dependent on those who brought him and on the mercy they implored. Bede had read the case spiritually (on v. 31): "he is deaf and dumb who neither has ears to hear the words of God, nor opens his mouth to speak them, and such must be presented to the Lord for healing by men who have already learned to hear and speak the divine oracles." The friends who carried this man to Christ become, in the witnesses' reading, a picture of the intercession by which the spiritually deaf are brought to the only Physician who can open their ears.
33:
"And he took him aside from the multitude, and put his fingers into his ears, and he spit, and touched his tongue." The witnesses dwell on the privacy of the cure and the meaning of the signs.

The taking aside the commentators read as both humility and tenderness. Gill says it was "to shun all appearance of ostentation and vain glory"; Pseudo-Chrysostom, that he might "not do his divine miracles openly, teaching us to cast away vain glory." Ellicott adds a gentler motive: "the same tender considerateness for the infirmities of the sufferer" — for "the man could not find in the pitying tones of the voice of the Healer that on which his faith could fasten, and the act came in to fill up the void." Robertson notes the secrecy served "partly to get the attention of the deaf and dumb" man, who "could not hear what Jesus said."

The fingers and the spittle puzzle the commentators, since a word would have sufficed. Barnes judges the sign "intended... simply as a sign that the power of healing came from Jesus" (cf. Mark 8:23; John 9:6). The fathers see more: Pseudo-Chrysostom, that Christ "might shew that his body, being united to Deity, was consecrated by Divine virtue, with all that he did," restoring in himself "the perfection of human nature" marred by Adam; Theophylact, "that he might shew that all the members of his sacred body are divine and holy, even the spittle."

Calvin cuts through the speculation. "There is no necessity for having recourse to allegories." The plain instruction is enough: "by touching the tongue with spittle, he intended to point out that the faculty of speech was communicated by himself alone; and by putting his fingers into the ears, he showed that it belonged to his office to pierce the ears of the deaf." The witnesses thus find in the unusual manner of the cure not magic but condescension: Christ stoops to the sufferer's weakness, giving the deaf man, through touch he could feel, the assurance that this power — over ears and tongue alike — came from no other than himself.
34:
"And looking up to heaven, he sighed, and saith unto him, Ephphatha, that is, Be opened." The witnesses dwell on the upward look, the sigh, and the word of power.

The look to heaven the commentators read as prayer and instruction. Gill says it was "not for assistance" but "for the sake of the man, to teach him that every good gift... was from above"; Barnes and Ellicott take it as "an act imploring aid from God" and "an attitude of prayer" (cf. John 11:41). Bede draws the two natures together: "looking up indeed into heaven as man, praying unto God, he groaned, but presently with one word, as being strong in the Divine Majesty, he healed."

The sigh the witnesses take as sympathy, not need. Pseudo-Chrysostom says he "groaned, as taking our cause upon himself and pitying human nature, seeing the misery into which it had fallen." Ellicott finds in it "the sadness of sympathy which we feel at the sight of suffering, even when we know that we have the power to remove its cause" (cf. John 11:33); Calvin, "an expression of strong feeling," revealing "the vehemence of his love towards men, for whose miseries he feels so much compassion."

The word "Ephphatha" Mark preserves in Aramaic — "another instance," Ellicott notes, "of St. Mark's reproduction of the very syllables uttered by our Lord." Its force the witnesses underline. Wesley calls it "a word of sovereign authority, not an address to God for power to heal: such an address was needless, for Christ had a perpetual fund of power residing in himself" (cf. John 5:21). Robertson notes the sigh as Jesus "felt a nervous strain in this complex case." The witnesses thus see the verse holding together the two truths the whole miracle displays: the true humanity that looks up and groans in fellow-feeling with human woe, and the true deity that needs only to say "Be opened," and it is done. Clarke notes the Evangelist kept the original word because the Lord "had pronounced it with peculiar and authoritative emphasis," its closing sound being one "that could not be expressed by any letter in the Greek alphabet" — a small token of the care with which Peter's recollection preserved the very speech of Jesus.
35:
"And straightway his ears were opened, and the string of his tongue was loosed, and he spake plain." The witnesses dwell on the immediacy and completeness of the cure and its fulfilment of prophecy.

The healing followed the word at once. Gill marvels that "such a power went along with the words of Christ, when he said, Be opened, that immediately... the man's hearing came to him, and he heard as quick as ever." The "ears" are, more exactly, "his hearings" — the plural "organs of hearing" (Ellicott, Gill), both ears restored. The "string of his tongue," Ellicott clarifies, is better "bond, that which confined and hampered his speech" (cf. Luke 13:16), with "no ground for thinking that St. Mark used the word in any anatomical sense."

The speech the commentators note was now correct. "He spake plain" — "distinctly, without stammering" (Clarke); Robertson reads the imperfect "he began to speak correctly" (orthōs), an inchoative tense marking the new and lasting power. Barnes simply: "the difficulty in his speaking was removed."

Above all the witnesses hear the echo of prophecy. Barnes points to Isaiah 35:5–6, "then the ears of the deaf shall be unstopped... and the tongue of the dumb sing." The very word Mark had chosen for the man's impediment (v. 32) had been drawn from that passage, so that the cure is its visible fulfilment: the Messiah who was to come "will come and save you... then shall the lame man leap, and the tongue of the dumb sing." The witnesses thus see in the opened ears and loosed tongue not merely a single act of mercy but a sign of the kingdom — the long-promised day in which the deaf hear and the dumb speak, accomplished in a moment by the word of Christ. What the man's friends had asked as a private favour proved a public token that the prophesied Saviour had come. Robertson notes the inchoative force of the tense — "he began to speak correctly" — marking not a momentary flush of sound but the settled possession of a faculty he had never rightly had; the cure was at once instantaneous and lasting.
36:
"And he charged them that they should tell no man: but the more he charged them, so much the more a great deal they published it." The witnesses dwell on the command of silence and the irrepressible report.

The charge belongs to the pattern of the Messianic secret. Barnes explains: "he was not ambitious of being known; and he knew that if much was said of his cures, it would excite the jealousy of the Pharisees, and endanger his life." Christ "charged them" — the man, his friends, and the witnesses (Gill) — to "tell no man."

Yet the command only multiplied the telling. Robertson notes the double comparative — "so much the more a great deal" — an imperfect of "continued action": "the more Jesus commanded them not to tell, the more they told. It was a continuous performance. Prohibitions always affect some people that way." Pseudo-Jerome had observed (on v. 31) that "a city placed on a hill cannot be hid," and "lowliness always comes before glory."

Calvin weighs the people's motive and Christ's purpose. He takes the command not as a secret encouragement to spread the fame — "many commentators torture these words to an opposite meaning" — but as a real intention "to delay the publication of it till a more proper and convenient time." Their "zeal was unseasonable, when, though forbidden to be silent, they were in haste to speak"; "we need not wonder that men unaccustomed to the doctrine of Christ are carried away by immoderate zeal." Yet "what they unwisely attempted to do was made by Christ to promote his own glory; for not only was the miracle made known, but the whole of that district, in despising the Author of heavenly gifts, was rendered inexcusable." The witnesses thus find in the scene both a rebuke to disordered zeal — which does the wrong thing from a right affection — and a comfort, that Christ overrules even our well-meant disobedience to the spread of his own honour. The same thing had happened with the cleansed leper (Mark 1:45), Robertson notes; the very command not to tell "provoked these people to tell," so that the fame of Jesus, which he sought to restrain for a season, broke out the more and filled the whole region.
37:
"And were beyond measure astonished, saying, He hath done all things well: he maketh both the deaf to hear, and the dumb to speak." The witnesses dwell on the people's amazement and the praise it produced.

The astonishment was unbounded. "Beyond measure," Barnes notes, is in the Greek "very abundantly"; the man cured, his friends, "and the whole multitude were exceedingly, beyond all expression, amazed" (Gill), for "there were other miracles also wrought at this time." Ellicott marks the contrast between Mark's "accurate description" of the man's ailment in v. 32 and "the less precise language of popular amazement" here.

Their cry "He hath done all things well" the commentators take as the heart of the verse. It means, says Barnes, "he has perfectly effected the cure of the deaf and the dumb" — "all things in a remarkable manner." Clarke expands it into a meditation: "this has been, and ever will be, true of every part of our Lord's conduct. In creation, providence, and redemption he hath done all things well." In creation "everything has been made in number, weight, and measure"; in providence "he preserves everything he has made"; and "how glorious does he appear in the work of redemption!" — "enough for all, a sufficiency for each, and an abundance for eternity."

The closing words echo the prophets and the Creator's prerogative. "He maketh both the deaf to hear, and the dumb to speak" recalls Isaiah 35:5–6 and the word of God to Moses, "Who hath made man's mouth?... have not I the Lord?" (Exodus 4:11). Gill draws the spiritual sense: "the grace of God, in opening the ears and heart of a sinner, and causing the tongue of the dumb to sing his praise, is very astonishing, to men and angels." The chapter that began with the Pharisees' fault-finding over unwashed hands thus ends with the wondering praise of a Gentile crowd — the proud critics silenced, and the simple amazed into confessing that this Healer had done all things well. Barnes observes that the cry was the spontaneous verdict of those who had seen, not of any partisan — a testimony wrung from a Gentile multitude by the sheer perfection of the work; and Clarke adds that in Christ "the good of the creature seems evidently more consulted than the glory of the Creator," though to the discerning eye both shine together in all he does.
